1: The daughters of Jerusalem's question — where has your beloved gone? — mirrors Song of Solomon 3:1-4 but at a more advanced stage: then the beloved searched alone; now she has companions who offer to search with her. Their offer to search with her (nevaqshennu immakh) uses the Piel plural — intensive, communal seeking. The community's inclusion in the search is a social affirmation of the beloved's love as something the community endorses and participates in.
2: לְגַנּוֹ (legano) — 'to his garden' — the beloved knows exactly where her beloved has gone because she IS his garden (Song of Solomon 4:12-16; Song of Solomon 5:1). The answer to the daughters' question is reflexive: he has gone to tend her. לִרְעוֹת בַּגַּנִּים (lir'ot bagganim) — 'to graze in the gardens' — the shepherd-beloved grazes among the lilies; both the shepherd metaphor and the garden metaphor resolve in the same place. לִלְקֹט שׁוֹשַׁנִּים (lilqot shoshanim) — 'to gather lilies' — the gathering of lilies echoes the beloved gathering spices (Song of Solomon 5:1); the action is complete and intimate.
3: אֲנִי לְדוֹדִי וְדוֹדִי לִי (ani ledodi vedodi li) — 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine' — the order reverses from Song of Solomon 2:16 (dodi li va'ani lo — my beloved is mine and I am his). The earlier formulation emphasized her claim on him; this formulation emphasizes her belonging to him before her claim on him. Scholars note the order's significance: maturation in the Song moves from self-assertion to self-giving. The phrase expresses the same mutual covenant formula but with a subtle deepening.
4: כְּתִרְצָה (ketirtzah) — 'beautiful as Tirzah' — Tirzah was the capital of the northern kingdom before Samaria (1 Kings 15:33; Song of Solomon 16:6), known for its beauty. The comparison places the beloved among the greatest cities of Israel. כִּירוּשָׁלַיִם (kiyerushalayim) — 'lovely as Jerusalem' — Jerusalem as the standard of civic beauty. אֲיֻמָּה כַּנִּדְגָּלוֹת (ayumah kannidgalot) — 'awe-inspiring as an army with banners' — ayom (awe-inspiring, terrible) describes the beloved's effect on observers: not merely beautiful but commanding, creating a mixture of attraction and reverent awe.
5: הַשִּׁיבִי עֵינַיִךְ מִנֶּגְדִּי (hashivi einayikh minnegdi) — 'look away; your eyes overwhelm me' — the same dove-eyes that were praised (Song of Solomon 1:15; Song of Solomon 4:1) now overwhelm the beloved. Rahavuni (they overwhelm me) from the root rahav (to press, overwhelm, behave proudly/arrogantly against); the eyes have a power that the lover finds too much to receive directly. The repetition of the hair-goats-Gilead comparison (= Song of Solomon 4:1) signals intentional refrain-use.
6: The repetition of the teeth comparison (4:2 verbatim) emphasizes the refrain-structure of the wasf sequences. The beloved's beauty is consistently described in the same terms — the repetition is not laziness but the artistic convention of the ancient love lyric: the repeated image becomes the signature description, as familiar as a beloved's name.
7: The pomegranate-cheeks comparison also repeats from Song of Solomon 4:3. The pomegranate (rimmon) was a symbol of fertility and abundance throughout the ancient Near East; its bright red interior suggests the warmth visible through the veil. The architectural detail (behind your veil, mibba'ad letzammatekh) frames the description in the same modest partially-revealed way.
8: שִׁשִּׁים הֵמָּה מְלָכוֹת (shishim hemah melakhot) — 'there may be sixty queens' — the numerical catalogue (sixty queens, eighty concubines, maidens without number) creates the social context against which the beloved is set. The numbers are not exact but rhetorical: a multitude of beautiful women whom a king might possess cannot compare with the uniquely valued one. The contrast anticipates the declaration of uniqueness in v9.
9: אַחַת הִיא יוֹנָתִי תַמָּתִי (achat hi yonati tammati) — 'she is unique — my dove, my perfect one' — achat (one, unique) is the declaration of incomparable singularity: she is the only one. Tammah (perfect, complete, whole) uses the same root as tom (integrity, wholeness) and tam (blameless, like Job). The beloved is declared perfect with the vocabulary of moral and ritual integrity. אַחַת הִיא לְאִמָּהּ (achat hi le'immah) — 'she is the only one of her mother' — her uniqueness is confirmed by her family origin.
10: מִי זֹאת (mi zot) — 'who is this?' — echoes 3:6 and Song of Solomon 8:5; the 'who is this?' question is the Song's way of marking the beloved's appearance as an event, an arrival that demands attention and identification. כַּשַּׁחַר (kashachar) — 'like the dawn' — shachar (dawn) is the beginning of light breaking through darkness; the beloved's appearance is compared to the first light of day. כַּחַמָּה (kechamah) — 'like the sun' — the full sun; כַּלְּבָנָה (kallevanah) — 'like the moon' — the full moon. Dawn, moon, sun — the complete range of luminaries — describe the beloved's radiance.
11: אֶל גִּנַּת אֱגוֹז יָרַדְתִּי (el ginnat egoz yaradti) — 'I went down to the walnut grove' — the only occurrence of egoz (walnut) in the Hebrew Bible. The walnut grove is a cultivated garden space; the going down (yarad) is a descent, perhaps to the lower garden terraces. לִרְאוֹת בְּאִבֵּי הַנָּחַל (lirot be'ibbei hanachal) — 'to look at the new growth in the valley' — ibbei (blossoms, new growth) from the root for budding; the valley's spring growth and the vineyard's blossoming are the evidence of the season's fruitfulness.
12: לֹא יָדַעְתִּי (lo yada'ti) — 'I did not know' — the sudden surprise of the verse; the speaker did not realize what was happening. This is one of the most difficult verses in the Song: the Hebrew nafshi samAtni markevot ammi nadiv is grammatically complex and debated. Translations range from 'before I realized it, my desire had set me among the chariots of my noble people' to 'I did not know it, but my soul had made me like the chariots of Amminadab.' The ambiguity may be intentional: the beloved is overcome by something she cannot fully articulate — desire placing her somewhere she did not consciously choose to be.
13: שׁוּבִי שׁוּבִי הַשּׁוּלַמִּית (shuvi shuvi hashulammit) — 'come back, come back, O Shulamite' — the only occurrence of Shulamit in the Song. Its meaning is debated: (1) a woman from Shunem (the town of Abishag, 1 Kings 1:3); (2) the feminine form of Solomon (Shelomoh), making her 'the Solomonic one' or 'the peaceful one'; (3) a term meaning 'the complete/whole one' (shalam = wholeness/peace). The quadruple shuvi (return, return, return, return) is an urgent, tender summons. The response question — why do you gaze on the Shulamite, as on the dance of the two camps? — introduces the dance imagery of Song of Solomon 7.
